title: Package Development
objectives: Distinguish a Dart package, Flutter plugin and FFI plugin
Structure and publish a reusable package
Apply versioning and good API design

sections.markdown: A package is reusable code published to pub.dev (or used locally). A pure Dart package has no platform code; a plugin adds native (platform-channel or FFI) implementations per platform.

Modern plugins are 'federated': a platform-interface package defines the contract, and each platform (android/ios/web) provides its own implementation package. This lets third parties add platform support without touching the core.
Use semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH): breaking changes bump MAJOR. A clear CHANGELOG, example app, tests, and good doc comments earn pub.dev points and adoption.
Design a small, stable public API; keep internals in src/ (not exported). Add example/, write tests, and run `dart pub publish --dry-run` before releasing.

flashcards.back: A package is pure Dart; a plugin adds native platform implementations (channels or FFI).
A platform-interface + separate per-platform implementation packages, so support can be added independently.
MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH — MAJOR for breaking changes, MINOR for features, PATCH for fixes.

quiz.explain: Plugins add native code; pure packages are Dart-only.
An interface package plus implementation packages per platform.
Semantic versioning increments MAJOR for breaking changes.

challenges.text: Create a pure Dart package with a tested utility function.
Build a Flutter plugin exposing one native method via a channel.
Make the plugin federated with a platform interface + Android/iOS impls.
Publish a package to pub.dev with example, tests, CI, and semantic versioning.

interviewQuestions: Package vs plugin vs FFI plugin?
What is a federated plugin and why?
How do you version and publish a package?
How do you design a good public API?
